title: Watch-to-Earn vs Play-to-Earn vs Move-to-Earn: The 2026 X-to-Earn Explainer
html:
If you have been comparing watch to earn vs play to earn, the short answer is this: both are branches of the same idea, "x-to-earn", where an app rewards a specific action with crypto instead of keeping all the value for itself. Watch-to-earn pays you for attention (watching videos, ads or live streams), play-to-earn pays you for playing games, and move-to-earn pays you for physical activity. The models share a structure but differ enormously in how well they hold up over time. This guide defines the category, compares the three main variants honestly, and explains why the ones tied to a real product tend to outlive the ones that are not.

What is x-to-earn?

X-to-earn is an umbrella term for any app that gives users an ownership stake or token reward for doing something they would probably do anyway. The "x" is a placeholder for the activity: watch, play, move, learn, create, and so on. The common thread is a shift in who captures the value. In a traditional platform, your attention and engagement are the product and the company keeps the upside. In an x-to-earn model, a share of that value is routed back to the people generating it.

That is the promise. The reality in 2026 is more nuanced, and it is worth being clear-eyed. The category has produced genuinely useful products and it has produced short-lived token schemes that collapsed once new deposits dried up. The difference almost always comes down to one question: where does the reward money actually come from? If it comes from real revenue (advertising, subscriptions, in-game spending, sponsorships), the model can last. If it comes only from the next wave of buyers, it cannot.

For a fuller primer on the attention side of this, see DeHub watch-to-earn explained.

Watch-to-earn: getting paid for attention

Watch-to-earn rewards you for viewing content: short videos, full-length uploads, adverts or live streams. It is the most intuitive branch of x-to-earn because the underlying economics already exist. Advertisers pay for attention today; watch-to-earn simply hands a slice of that spend to the viewer rather than pocketing all of it.

The honest case for it. Viewing is a low-effort, high-frequency activity, so the barrier to entry is near zero. You do not need to be good at a game or own expensive kit. When rewards are funded by genuine ad spend or creator subscriptions, the loop is sustainable because value is flowing in from outside the token.

The honest limits. Per-view rewards are small by nature. Nobody replaces a salary by watching clips, and any platform promising otherwise should raise an eyebrow. The risk to watch for is "watch2earn vs play2earn" schemes that inflate rewards with freshly minted tokens and no external revenue; those inevitably dilute. We unpack the warning signs in is watch-to-earn legit.

On DeHub, watch-to-earn takes the form of watch-to-earn bounties attached to content, funded through the platform's advertising model, which shares up to 90% of ad revenue back to the ecosystem. Because the money originates from advertisers rather than from new token buyers, the incentive is grounded in real demand.

Play-to-earn: getting paid for playing

Play-to-earn (P2E) rewards you for progressing in a game: completing matches, winning tournaments, or earning and trading in-game assets. It was the branch that first made x-to-earn famous, and also the branch that produced the most cautionary tales.

The honest case for it. Games are sticky. People play for enjoyment first, which means engagement does not depend solely on the payout. A well-designed P2E title can reward skill and time with assets that hold value because other players actually want them. That is a healthier foundation than pure yield.

The honest limits. The first-generation P2E boom leaned heavily on "infinite mint" tokenomics, where rewards were paid in a token that only had value while new players kept buying in. When growth stalled, the rewards collapsed, and with them the games. The lesson the sector absorbed is that the game has to be worth playing even if the token went to zero.

This is exactly why the industry has been shifting from classic play-to-earn towards play-and-own, the maturing model where you genuinely own your in-game items as tradable assets rather than chasing an emissions-based yield. In a "play to earn vs play-and-own" comparison, play-and-own puts the fun and the ownership first and treats earnings as a consequence of real demand, not the whole point.

DeHub's flagship game Last Chad Standing sits in this camp: an MMA-style battle royale with licensed fighters, built as a game people want to play, with earnings layered on top. You can read the full breakdown in the Last Chad Standing play-to-earn guide, and if you want to start without spending anything, our roundup of free play-to-earn games with no investment is the place to begin.

Move-to-earn: getting paid for activity

Move-to-earn (M2E) rewards physical movement: walking, running or cycling, tracked by an app and converted into tokens or in-app rewards. It had a striking rise in the early 2020s off the back of one or two headline apps.

The honest case for it. The behaviour it encourages is unambiguously positive, and there is real-world value in fitness and wellness data and partnerships. When rewards are funded by brand sponsorship, premium subscriptions or a healthcare/insurance tie-in, there is a plausible external revenue source.

The honest limits. Move-to-earn has faced the toughest sustainability test of the three. Many apps required an upfront purchase (special NFT trainers, for example) before you could earn, which made them look uncomfortably like a pay-to-play scheme dressed as fitness. Verifying real movement (rather than a phone taped to a metronome) is also genuinely hard. The M2E projects still standing in 2026 tend to be the ones that de-emphasised speculative earnings and leaned into fitness as the actual product.

Watch-to-earn vs play-to-earn: which model lasts?

Across all three variants, the sustainability test is identical. Ask three questions of any x-to-earn app:

  • Where does the reward money come from? External revenue (ads, subscriptions, sponsorship, in-game spending) is sustainable. Rewards paid only from new deposits are not.
  • Is the core activity worth doing without the reward? A good game, a feed worth watching, or exercise you would do anyway. If the reward is the only reason anyone shows up, the model is fragile.
  • Do you actually own what you earn? Real, tradable ownership (the play-and-own principle) beats a balance the platform can inflate or switch off.

Judged this way, watch-to-earn and mature play-and-own tend to score best, because both can be funded by revenue that already exists in the wider media and gaming economy. Move-to-earn can work but has a narrower path. None of them is a shortcut to serious income; the realistic framing is "earn crypto watching and playing" as a bonus layered on top of things you enjoy, not a wage.

Where DeHub fits: watch and play under one roof

Most x-to-earn apps do exactly one thing. DeHub is unusual in offering both watch-to-earn and play-to-earn inside a single platform, alongside creator tools, live streaming and on-chain tradable subscriptions. That matters for sustainability: the rewards are spread across multiple real revenue sources (advertising, tokenised uploads, subscriptions and games) rather than a single token faucet.

It is built as DeHub, the decentralised social network, so the "earn" sits on top of a product people use for its own sake, which is precisely the structure the healthiest x-to-earn models share. If you want to try it, the DeHub app is live on the web and on Google Play.

Frequently asked questions

What is x-to-earn in simple terms?

X-to-earn is any app that rewards a specific action ("x") with crypto or ownership instead of keeping all the value itself. Watch-to-earn, play-to-earn and move-to-earn are the three best-known variants. The strongest examples fund rewards from real revenue rather than from new buyers.

Watch-to-earn vs play-to-earn: which earns more?

Neither is a reliable income and any claim otherwise should be treated with caution. Watch-to-earn offers small, frequent rewards for near-zero effort, while play-to-earn can pay more per session but depends on the game's quality and demand for its assets. Sustainability matters more than headline numbers.

What is play-and-own and how is it different from play-to-earn?

Play-and-own is the maturing version of play-to-earn. Instead of chasing an emissions-based token yield, you genuinely own your in-game items as tradable assets. The game is designed to be enjoyable first, so it does not collapse the moment reward rates fall.

Is move-to-earn still worth it in 2026?

Move-to-earn can be worthwhile where the app treats fitness as the real product and funds rewards through sponsorship or subscriptions. Be wary of any model that requires a large upfront purchase before you can earn, and expect modest rewards rather than meaningful income.

Can one platform do both watch-to-earn and play-to-earn?

Yes. DeHub offers watch-to-earn bounties and play-to-earn games (including Last Chad Standing) within one platform, funded from several real revenue streams. Spreading rewards across advertising, subscriptions and games is generally healthier than relying on a single token.
